nativeRange.habitat: Found in large flowing rivers and seasonally flooded fields, where it takes refuge among submerged rocks and tree roots during daylight hours. The species is strongly migratory, undertaking extensive seasonal spawning migrations upstream and downstream in response to changes in water level.

decorPreferences: driftwood and root tangles for refuge
caves and overhangs (PVC tubes, rock piles)
moderate current to replicate river conditions
open swimming corridors between shelter zones

diet.notes: The Redtail Botia is an opportunistic bottom-feeder with a strong preference for meaty and invertebrate foods. In the wild it forages for aquatic snails, worms, insect larvae, and small crustaceans, which makes it a natural and effective pest-snail controller in the aquarium. In captivity it readily accepts high-quality sinking pellets, frozen or live bloodworm, Tubifex, brine shrimp, daphnia, and chopped earthworm, and will also take blanched vegetables such as cucumber, zucchini, and spinach, as well as algae wafers. Because the species is nocturnal, offering food after lights-out gives it the best opportunity to feed undisturbed. Variety in the diet is important; feeding a single food type over a long period tends to lead to nutritional deficiency.

goodWith: large active cyprinids (tiger barbs, tinfoil barbs, giant danios)
other mid-to-upper-water fish of similar or larger size
robust cichlids of comparable size (with caution)
avoidWith: small fish that can be bullied or eaten
other bottom-dwellers competing for the same territory and food
invertebrates — shrimp and snails will be consumed
peaceful community fish unsuited to semi-aggressive tankmates

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Lerdsuwa, 1700x1133. Sharp full-body lateral view of a live Yasuhikotakia modesta showing the blue-grey body, mouth barbels and correct loach profile; caudal fin only faintly reddish (drab captive specimen), but the clearest, highest-resolution live shot available under a free licence.

Photo by Lerdsuwa, 1024x682. Live full-body side view, correct ID (barbels, blue-grey body); slightly softer than the recommended pick and tail also drab rather than vivid red. Kept as a second angle.

summary: The Redtail Botia is a large loach from Southeast Asian river systems, recognized by its blue-grey body and vivid orange-red caudal fin. Known also as the Blue Botia (Yasuhikotakia modesta), it is an effective natural snail controller and a gregarious shoaler that requires groups of five or more and a tank of at least 300 litres.
careNotes:
The Redtail Botia was long sold under the older name Botia modesta, and that name is still common in the aquarium trade and older literature. In the early 2000s, taxonomic work reorganised the Botia group — which had become a catch-all genus containing unrelated species — and Yasuhikotakia modesta was established as the current accepted name. The older synonym Botia rubripinnis also refers to the same fish.

This is a large, robust loach reaching up to 25 cm total length in the wild, though aquarium specimens typically reach 18–22 cm. A tank of at least 300 L with a 150 cm footprint is the generally accepted minimum for a shoal of five to six adults, and larger is strongly recommended. Tight social groups of at least five individuals — ideally ten or more — are essential: undersized groups tend to intensify aggression and stress among the fish, while a larger shoal distributes the social hierarchy and produces calmer, bolder behaviour.

The species is nocturnal by nature, spending daylight hours concealed within caves, root tangles, or rock piles. Dense structured decor with multiple retreat zones is important; bare or sparsely decorated tanks are likely to cause chronic stress. A moderate current that echoes the riverine habitat is beneficial. Sand or smooth fine gravel at least 5 cm deep is strongly preferred, since sharp substrate can damage the sensitive barbels (the whisker-like sensory organs around the mouth).

The Redtail Botia is a reliable snail-eater and is often introduced specifically to control pest snail populations such as Melanoides trumpet snails, ramshorns, and pond snails. Other invertebrates, including shrimp, should not be kept with this species.

Like all loaches, the Redtail Botia is sensitive to poor water quality and to copper-based medications, which are commonly used to treat ich and other diseases but can be lethal to scaleless fish. New specimens should be quarantined carefully — ich and external parasites are common on wild-caught imports — and any medication should be used at half the standard dose. Captive breeding has not been reliably achieved without hormonal induction.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is considered practically impossible under normal aquarium conditions. In the wild, Yasuhikotakia modesta undertakes extensive seasonal migrations — moving upstream from roughly November through March, then back downstream from May through July in the lower Mekong system — to spawn in flooded forests and riverside fields during the rainy season. Commercial aquaculture relies on hormonal induction to trigger spawning, and no reliable accounts of private aquarists breeding this species without such intervention are known. Sexing outside of spawning condition is difficult; ripe females may appear noticeably broader through the abdomen.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Yasuhikotakia modesta
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (200–250 mm SL), temperature (23–28°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (36–215 ppm ≈ 2–12 dGH), native range (Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, Vietnam; Chao Phraya, Mae Klong, Mekong drainages), tank minimum (150 × 45 cm base), habitat, diet, breeding notes on migratory spawning, and social requirements (groups of 5–6 minimum, 10+ preferred). Notes Botia modesta and Botia rubripinnis as synonyms; cites Nalbant 2002 reclassification to Yasuhikotakia.

additional.name: FishBase — Yasuhikotakia modesta
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (25 cm SL), temperature (26–30°C), taxonomy (Botiidae, Cypriniformes), native range (Mekong, Chao Phraya, Mae Khlong basins), habitat (large rivers with muddy substrate, flooded fields), diet (nocturnal predator: worms, crustaceans, insects), and captive lifespan (8+ years reported). Confirmed strongly migratory behaviour.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Blue Botia (Yasuhikotakia modesta)
additional.notes: Supplementary care guide. Confirmed temperature 22–30°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 8–12 dGH, minimum 75-gallon tank (≈284 L) for adults, shoal of 5–10 required, lifespan approximately 5 years in captivity with proper care, sensitivity to medications (scaleless fish), compatibility guidance (large active cyprinids only).

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping — Redtail Botia Loach
additional.notes: Confirmed Yasuhikotakia modesta as accepted name (formerly Botia modesta), native range lower Mekong basin plus Chao Phraya and Mae Klong drainages, semi-aggressive temperament, groups of 6–8+ minimum, lifespan 5–7 years, diet (snails, invertebrates, insects, worms as natural prey), not suitable for peaceful community tanks, commercial hormone breeding.

additional.name: AquaInfo — Yasuhikotakia modesta Redtail Botia
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size up to 24 cm, temperature 24–28°C, pH 6.0–7.0, minimum tank length 150 cm, groups of at least 6, synonyms Botia modesta and Botia rubripinnis, native range including Chao Phraya, Great Lake (Tonle Sap), Mae Klong, Mekong, and Tonle Sap ecosystems.

fish.breedingNotes: Seasonal migratory spawner; captive breeding without hormonal induction is unknown. Commercial aquaculture uses hormone injection. Wild fish migrate extensively to spawn in flooded forests during the rainy season. Sexing is difficult outside of breeding condition.
